summary: Then, around 2200 BCE, the world fell over — and this time the witnesses aren't only the texts; they're the cities themselves. Within the same short window, the Akkadian Empire, Egypt's Old Kingdom, the great Indus Valley cities, and China's Liangzhu culture all collapsed. Four civilizations on three continents, no contact between them, ending at once. The texts blame the gods and the drying of the land; the soil cores agree there was a brutal, worldwide drought. An age simply stopped.

sources.textRecorded: Sumerian literary text, copies c. 2100–1800 BCE
sources.provenance: One of the best-attested Sumerian literary works.
sources.quote: The text records how the mighty empire of Akkad, under Naram-Sin, was struck down by the angered gods: 'for the first time since cities were built and founded, the great agricultural tracts produced no grain… he who slept on the roof, died on the roof.' Famine, empty markets and chaos end the world's first empire.
sources.translation: Summarized from the Curse of Agade (ETCSL 2.1.5).
sources.citation: The Cursing of Agade (ETCSL t.2.1.5), lines on the famine of Akkad.
sources.notes: The first empire in history remembers its own fall as a divine judgment of drought and famine — dated to the same 2200 BCE window as everyone else's collapse.

sources.work: The Admonitions of Ipuwer; the First Intermediate Period
sources.textRecorded: papyrus copy later; events at the Old Kingdom's fall
sources.provenance: Egypt; the collapse of pyramid-age central authority.
sources.quote: Lo, the desert claims the land, the nomes are laid waste… Lo, the river is blood, yet men drink of it… Lo, gates, columns and walls are burnt… The land is diminished, its rulers are multiplied.
sources.translation: After A. H. Gardiner's edition of the Admonitions (1909), public domain.
sources.citation: The Admonitions of Ipuwer (Papyrus Leiden I 344); cf. the First Intermediate Period.
sources.notes: Egypt's Old Kingdom — the age of the Great Pyramid — collapses into famine and anarchy at the same moment, its lament reading like the end of the world.

sources.provenance: Mohenjo-daro, Harappa and the Indus cities.
sources.quote: The largest and most advanced urban civilization of its day — planned cities, grid streets, plumbing — began to empty out in the same centuries, as the monsoon weakened and rivers shifted, and its people dispersed eastward.
sources.translation: Summarized from the archaeology of the Harappan decline.
sources.citation: Indus Valley Civilization decline, c. 2200–1900 BCE.
sources.notes: No surviving texts to read — but the abandoned cities are the testimony, and they empty in the same window as Akkad and Egypt.

sources.quote: China's sophisticated Liangzhu culture — famous for jade and vast hydraulic earthworks — vanished around 2300–2200 BCE, with sediment evidence pointing to catastrophic flooding and climate upheaval. Chinese tradition then remembers the Great Flood and Yu, who masters the waters and founds the first dynasty.
sources.translation: Summarized from the archaeology of Liangzhu and Chinese flood tradition.
sources.citation: Liangzhu culture collapse, c. 2300–2200 BCE; cf. the flood of Yao/Yu.
sources.notes: The eastern bookend: a great culture wiped out in the same window, on the far side of Asia — followed by China's own flood-and-renewal story.

sources.textRecorded: ice cores, lake beds, cave formations
sources.quote: Independent of any text, the earth itself recorded a sudden, severe, centuries-long dry spell around 2200 BCE — preserved in ice cores, dried lake beds and cave stalagmites worldwide. Geologists named our current age, the Meghalayan, from this very event. The texts and the planet are describing the same catastrophe.
sources.notes: This is the hinge of the whole chapter: the simultaneous collapses aren't a coincidence of dates — the ground confirms a real, global climate catastrophe at exactly that time.

sources.textRecorded: Völuspá c. 900–1100 CE; Snorri Sturluson's Prose Edda c. 1220 CE
sources.provenance: The Norse account of the age-ending collapse before Ragnarök; the Völuspá ('Seeress's Prophecy') is one of the most important surviving Norse poems.
sources.quote: Before Ragnarök comes Fimbulwinter — three successive winters with no summer between them. 'Snow drives from all directions; the winds are harsh and the world has no mercy.' The sun gives no warmth; the earth is gripped in cold; famine sweeps the land; kinship bonds dissolve and wars break out everywhere — 'an axe-age, a sword-age, a wolf-age.' The age collapses from within, undone by cold and chaos, before the gods themselves fall.
sources.translation: Summarized from Völuspá, stanzas 41–44; Prose Edda, Gylfaginning 51; translations by Lee Hollander and Arthur Gilchrist Brodeur, public domain.
sources.citation: Völuspá, stanzas 41–44; Prose Edda, Gylfaginning 51 (Fimbulwinter and Ragnarök).
sources.notes: The Norse memory of a catastrophic multi-year winter bringing civilizational collapse. Scholars have linked Fimbulwinter to the volcanic winters of 536–541 CE (which tree-ring records show caused global crop failure and famine) or to older climate-collapse memories. Either way, the same pattern: an age ends in cold, famine, and the dissolution of social order.

sources.work: The Kali Yuga and Mahapralaya — Mahabharata; Vishnu Purana
sources.textRecorded: Mahabharata c. 4th c. BCE – 4th c. CE; Vishnu Purana c. 1st–4th c. CE
sources.provenance: The Hindu cosmological cycle of four ages (yugas), culminating in dissolution and reset, is one of the most detailed ancient frameworks for thinking about the end of a world-age.
sources.quote: The Puranas describe history as a cycle of four declining ages — Satya, Treta, Dvapara and Kali Yuga. At the Kali Yuga's end comes Mahapralaya (the great dissolution): seven suns blaze at once and parch the earth; catastrophic floods follow; the world is dissolved and remade. In the Kali age preceding the collapse: 'the world will be filled with thieves; rulers will confiscate property… rains will be scanty; plants will not fruit… famines will devastate the earth.' The great teachers withdraw; dharma collapses; famine, plague and war rule — and then the age resets.
sources.translation: Summarized from Vishnu Purana 6.1–4 (Pralaya) and the Mahabharata, Vana Parva 188–189 (Markandeya's account of the Kali Yuga).
sources.citation: Vishnu Purana 6.1–4 (Mahapralaya); Mahabharata, Vana Parva 188–189 (the four yugas); cf. Bhagavata Purana 12.2.
sources.notes: India encodes the memory of catastrophic age-endings as a cosmological cycle. Each transition matches the same pattern: climate collapse, drought, social dissolution, and a reset. The Hindu tradition placed the Dvapara–Kali transition at approximately 3100 BCE — within the range of the earliest Bronze Age civilizations and the general period when the oldest written records begin.

science.intro: This chapter barely needs a defense — the science is settled and it agrees with the texts: around 2200 BCE the climate broke and the first civilizations fell together. Geologists even named our current age after it.

evidence.title: The earth logged the drought itself
evidence.observation: Ice cores, lake beds and cave stalagmites worldwide record a sudden, severe, centuries-long dry spell beginning ~2200 BCE — the '4.2-kiloyear event.'
evidence.tie: A real, global catastrophe at exactly the moment the texts say an age ended — recorded by the planet, not by people.

evidence.title: Geology named an age after it
evidence.observation: In 2018 the international geological body defined the current 'Meghalayan Age' as beginning with this 2200 BCE climate collapse — its boundary marked in an Indian stalagmite.
evidence.tie: The event is so real and so global it's a formal chapter boundary in the earth's official timeline.

evidence.title: Four civilizations fell at once
evidence.observation: The Akkadian Empire, Egypt's Old Kingdom, the Indus Valley cities and China's Liangzhu culture all collapsed within the same ~2200 BCE window, on three continents.
evidence.tie: Simultaneous, unconnected collapse worldwide — exactly 'the age that ended at once.'
evidence.source: Bronze Age collapse cluster; Tell Leilan abandonment (Weiss et al.).
